prevalence.source: Dermatology epidemiology syntheses; Brazilian dermatology practice patterns
prevalence.trend: stable; adult acne recognized more frequently

burden.dalyPerCapita: low mortality; meaningful mental health/QOL impact (scarring, stigma)
burden.source: IHME Global Burden of Disease (skin conditions) context

vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to dermatology care
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis based on comedones, papules/pustules, nodules and scarring; assess severity and psychosocial impact; evaluate hyperandrogenism when indicated

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; symptom-driven care (often primary care/dermatology)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; improves with adherence and access to topical/systemic therapies

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Clinical diagnosis; specialist referral for severe nodulocystic acne or scarring
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Topicals (benzoyl peroxide, retinoids), topical/oral antibiotics when indicated, hormonal therapy in selected women, isotretinoin for severe cases with monitoring
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Access to dermatology and consistent follow-up; isotretinoin monitoring capacity varies; misinformation and overuse of antibiotics
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Broad availability of effective topical therapies and generics; dermatology expertise concentrated in major centers
